questions.question:
An element in an array X is called a leader if it is greater than all elements to the right of it in X. The best algorithm to find all leaders in an array:

A. solves it in linear time using a left to right pass of the array
B. solves it in linear time using a right to left pass of the array
C. solves it using divide and conquer in time Theta(n log n)
D. solves it in time Theta(n^2)
questions.answer: B. solves it in linear time using a right to left pass of the array
questions.to_find: The most efficient algorithm to find all leader elements in an array

understand.plain: A leader element is one that is strictly greater than every element that appears after it in the array. We want to find all such elements as efficiently as possible. By scanning from right to left and tracking the current maximum seen so far, we can identify leaders in a single O(n) pass.

keywords.explain: An element X[i] such that X[i] > X[j] for all j > i (every element to its right is smaller).
keywords.example: In [16, 17, 4, 3, 5, 2], leaders are 17, 5, 2 (rightmost is always a leader).

keywords.explain: Scanning the array starting from the last element towards the first, maintaining the maximum value seen so far.
keywords.example: Scan [3, 1, 4, 2]: start at 2 (leader, max=2), then 4 > 2 (leader, max=4), then 1 not > 4, then 3 not > 4.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 560 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='560' height='180' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='280' y='30' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Array: [16, 17, 4, 3, 5, 2]</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='70' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='55' y='82' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>16</text><rect x='100' y='50' width='70' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='135' y='82' class='lbl ink' class='bg'/><text x='295' y='82' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>3</text><rect x='340' y='50' width='70' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='375' y='82' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>5</text><rect x='420' y='50' width='70' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='455' y='82' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>2</text><text x='55' y='130' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>not leader</text><text x='135' y='130' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>LEADER</text><text x='215' y='130' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>not leader</text><text x='295' y='130' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>not leader</text><text x='375' y='130' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>LEADER</text><text x='455' y='130' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>LEADER</text><text x='280' y='165' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Right-to-left scan: track max seen so far</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array [16, 17, 4, 3, 5, 2] with leaders 17, 5, and 2 highlighted. A right-to-left scan tracks the running maximum to identify leaders in O(n).

given.aim: Find all leader elements (greater than all elements to the right) in an array as efficiently as possible.

terms.meaning: X[i] > X[j] for all j > i
terms.example: In [5, 3, 4, 2], element 4 is a leader because 4 > 2.
terms.connects: Defines which elements we need to report.

terms.meaning: The largest element encountered while scanning from the right end toward the left.
terms.example: Scanning [5, 3, 4, 2] right-to-left: max starts at 2, then 4 updates max to 4, 3 not > 4, 5 > 4 so leader.
terms.connects: An element X[i] is a leader if and only if X[i] > current running max from its right.

terms.meaning: The algorithm visits each element exactly once, making n comparisons total.
terms.example: One pass through n elements = O(n) time.
terms.connects: Right-to-left scan achieves this optimal complexity; left-to-right would require knowing all future elements.

given.plan: Scan the array from right to left. Maintain a variable 'max_so_far' initialized to the rightmost element (which is always a leader). For each element going left, if it exceeds max_so_far, it is a leader — report it and update max_so_far. This is a single O(n) pass.

steps.title: Eliminate left-to-right approach
steps.apply: A left-to-right scan cannot determine if an element is a leader without knowing all elements to its right. You would either need O(n) extra computation per element (making it O(n^2)) or pre-processing. So option A (left-to-right in linear time) is incorrect as stated for a naive approach.
steps.note: The rightmost element is always a leader — this naturally fits right-to-left traversal.

steps.title: Right-to-left linear scan
steps.formula_raw: if X[i] > max_right then X[i] is leader; max_right = X[i]
steps.apply: Initialize max_right = X[n-1], report X[n-1] as leader. For i from n-2 down to 0: if X[i] > max_right, report X[i] as a leader and set max_right = X[i]. This single pass takes O(n) time and O(1) extra space (apart from output). Example on [16,17,4,3,5,2]: scan right to left: 2 (leader, max=2), 5>2 (leader, max=5), 3 not >5, 4 not >5, 17>5 (leader, max=17), 16 not >17. Leaders = {17, 5, 2}.
steps.note: At each step, max_right records the maximum of all elements to the right of the current position, enabling O(1) leader check per element.

solution.result: The best algorithm solves it in linear time using a right to left pass of the array. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Single-pass right-to-left scan with a running maximum; each element is checked once in O(1) giving overall O(n) time.

questions.question:
Given two arrays of numbers a_1, ..., a_n and b_1, ..., b_n where each number is 0 or 1, the fastest algorithm to find the largest span (i, j) such that a_i + a_{i+1} + ... + a_j = b_i + b_{i+1} + ... + b_j or report that there is no such span,

A. Takes O(3^n) and Omega(2^n) time if hashing is permitted
B. Takes O(n) and Omega(n) time in the key comparison mode
C. Takes Theta(n) time and space
D. Takes O(n) time only if the sum of the 2n elements is an even number
questions.answer: C. Takes Theta(n) time and space
questions.to_find: The time and space complexity of the fastest algorithm to find the largest span (i,j) where the sub-array sums of two 0/1 arrays are equal

understand.plain: We have two arrays a and b, each containing only 0s and 1s. We want to find the longest contiguous subarray (from index i to j) where the number of 1s in a equals the number of 1s in b for that range. The key insight is to work with the difference array d[k] = a[k] - b[k], which is in {-1, 0, 1}, and find the longest subarray with sum zero.

keywords.meaning: A contiguous range of indices from i to j inclusive.
keywords.example: Span (2, 5) in array [1,0,1,0,1,1] covers elements at positions 2,3,4,5.

keywords.meaning: Create d[k] = a[k] - b[k]. The condition sum(a[i..j]) = sum(b[i..j]) is equivalent to sum(d[i..j]) = 0.
keywords.example: a=[1,0,1], b=[0,1,1]: d=[1,-1,0]. We need subarray of d with sum 0.

keywords.meaning: Compute prefix sums P[0..n] where P[0]=0 and P[k]=d[1]+...+d[k]. Span (i,j) has sum 0 iff P[j]=P[i-1]. Track first occurrence of each prefix sum in a hash map.
keywords.example: If P[2]=P[7], then span (3,7) has zero sum, giving length 5.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 560 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='560' height='200' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='280' y='28' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Algorithm: Largest Equal-Sum Span</text><text x='20' y='60' class='sub ink'>Step 1: Build d[k] = a[k] - b[k]  (each d[k] in {-1, 0, 1})</text><text x='20' y='85' class='sub ink'>Step 2: Compute prefix sums P[0..n], P[0] = 0</text><text x='20' y='110' class='sub ink'>Step 3: Store first occurrence of each prefix value in hash map</text><text x='20' y='135' class='sub ink'>Step 4: For each P[j], find earliest P[i-1] = P[j] => span (i,j)</text><text x='20' y='160' class='sub ink'>Step 5: Track maximum (j - i + 1) over all valid pairs</text><text x='20' y='185' class='sub ok'>Result: O(n) time, O(n) space for hash map => Theta(n) both</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Five-step algorithm using difference array and prefix sums with a hash map to find the largest equal-sum span in Theta(n) time and space.

given.aim: Find the largest index span (i,j) such that the subarray sum of a equals subarray sum of b, or determine no such span exists.

terms.term: Arrays a and b of length n with 0/1 entries
terms.meaning: Each element of a and b is either 0 or 1.
terms.connects: The 0/1 constraint means d[k] = a[k]-b[k] in {-1,0,1}, keeping prefix sums bounded.

terms.meaning: A derived array whose subarray sums are zero exactly when a and b have equal sums over that range.
terms.connects: Reduces the problem to 'longest subarray with sum 0' which is solved by prefix sum hashing.

terms.meaning: Hash map storing the first index at which each prefix sum value appears; if the same prefix sum reappears, a zero-sum subarray exists between those indices.
terms.example: P=[0,1,0,1,0]: P[0]=P[2]=P[4]=0, so longest zero-sum subarray ends at index 4, starts at index 1, length=4.
terms.connects: Enables O(1) lookup per element, giving O(n) total time with O(n) space for the map.

given.plan: Construct the difference array d[k]=a[k]-b[k]. Compute prefix sums P[0..n]. Use a hash map to record the first occurrence index of each prefix value. For every index j, check if P[j] appeared before; if so, the span between those indices is a valid candidate. Return the maximum such span.

steps.title: Reduce to longest zero-sum subarray
steps.formula_raw: sum(a[i..j]) = sum(b[i..j]) <=> sum(d[i..j]) = 0, where d[k] = a[k] - b[k]
steps.apply: Create d[k] = a[k] - b[k] for k=1 to n. Now the problem is: find the largest span (i,j) such that d[i] + d[i+1] + ... + d[j] = 0. Each d[k] is in {-1, 0, 1}.
steps.note: This classic reduction converts a two-array problem into a single-array sum problem.

steps.title: Solve using prefix sums and a hash map
steps.formula_raw: P[j] - P[i-1] = 0 => P[i-1] = P[j]; span length = j - i + 1
steps.apply: Initialize P[0]=0. Compute P[k] = P[k-1] + d[k] for k=1..n. Maintain a hash map 'first' where first[v] = smallest index r such that P[r] = v. For each j from 0 to n: if P[j] is in first, best_span = max(best_span, j - first[P[j]]); else first[P[j]] = j. The hash map stores at most n+1 entries (O(n) space). Each element processed in O(1) average time, giving O(n) total time.
steps.note: Options A and B are obviously wrong (exponential / no-hashing claims are false). Option D is incorrect because the algorithm works regardless of whether the total sum is even. Option C (Theta(n) time and space) correctly describes this approach.

solution.result: The fastest algorithm takes Theta(n) time and space (using difference array + prefix sums + hashing). Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Reduction to 'longest subarray with sum zero' via difference array, solved by prefix sum hashing in Theta(n) time and O(n) space.

questions.question: There are 5 bags labeled 1 to 5. All the coins in a given bag have the same weight. Some bags have coins of weight 10 gm, others have coins of weight 11 gm. I pick coins 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 coins respectively from bags 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Their total weight comes out to 323 gm. Then the product of the labels of the bags having 11 gm coins is ___.
questions.to_find: The product of the labels of bags that contain 11 gm coins

understand.plain: We have 5 bags labeled 1 through 5. Each bag contains only one type of coin — either 10 gm or 11 gm. We take a specific number of coins from each bag (1, 2, 4, 8, 16 — powers of 2) and weigh them together. The excess weight above the 10 gm baseline tells us exactly which bags have 11 gm coins, because the picks are distinct powers of 2.

keywords.meaning: By picking 2^(k-1) coins from bag k, each bag contributes a unique power of 2 to the excess weight. The excess uniquely identifies which bags have 11 gm coins.
keywords.example: Bags with 11 gm coins contribute 1 extra gm per coin. Bag 1 contributes 1 extra, bag 2 contributes 2 extra, bag 3 contributes 4 extra, etc.

keywords.meaning: The weight if ALL coins were 10 gm: sum of picks * 10.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 580 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='580' height='220' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='290' y='30' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Bag Analysis</text><rect x='10' y='45' width='100' height='40' class='hi'/><rect x='120' y='45' width='100' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='230' y='45' width='100' height='40' class='hi'/><rect x='340' y='45' width='100' height='40' class='hi'/><rect x='450' y='45' width='110' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='72' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Bag 1</text><text x='170' y='72' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Bag class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>11 gm</text><text x='390' y='110' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>11 gm</text><text x='505' y='110' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>10 gm</text><text x='60' y='135' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>pick: 1</text><text x='170' y='135' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>pick: 2</text><text x='280' y='135' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>pick: 4</text><text x='390' y='135' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>pick: 8</text><text x='505' y='135' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>pick: 16</text><text x='60' y='158' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>+1 extra</text><text x='170' y='158' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>+0 extra</text><text x='280' y='158' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>+4 extra</text><text x='390' y='158' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>+8 extra</text><text x='505' y='158' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>+0 extra</text><text x='290' y='185' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Excess = 323 - 310 = 13 = 1 + 4 + 8 (bags 1,3,4)</text><text x='290' y='210' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>Product of labels = 1 x 3 x 4 = 12</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing 5 bags with their coin types (11gm for bags 1,3,4 and 10gm for bags 2,5), picks (1,2,4,8,16), and extra weight contribution. Excess 13 = 1+4+8 identifies bags 1,3,4 with 11gm coins, product = 12.

given.aim: Identify which bags contain 11 gm coins from the total weight, and compute the product of their labels.

terms.meaning: We take 2^(k-1) coins from bag k, giving each bag a unique power-of-2 contribution to any excess weight.
terms.example: Bag 3 contributes 4 coins; if it has 11gm coins, it adds 4 extra grams.
terms.connects: Powers of 2 ensure the excess uniquely decodes which bags are 11gm (like binary representation).

terms.meaning: (1+2+4+8+16) * 10 = 31 * 10 = 310 gm — weight if all bags had 10 gm coins.
terms.example: If all bags had 10gm coins, total = 310 gm.
terms.connects: Subtracting from actual total gives the excess attributable to 11gm bags.

terms.meaning: 323 - 310 = 13 extra grams, each coming from the 11gm bags contributing 1 extra gm per coin.
terms.example: Excess 13 = 8 + 4 + 1, matching picks from bags 4, 3, and 1.
terms.connects: Decomposing 13 as sum of distinct powers of 2 gives the set of 11gm bags.

given.plan: Compute baseline weight (all 10gm) = 310. Excess = 323 - 310 = 13. Express 13 as a sum of distinct powers of 2 from {1,2,4,8,16}: 13 = 8+4+1. These correspond to bags 4, 3, 1. Multiply their labels: 1 * 3 * 4 = 12.

steps.title: Compute baseline and excess weight
steps.apply: If every bag had 10 gm coins: total = (1+2+4+8+16)*10 = 31*10 = 310 gm. Actual total = 323 gm. Excess = 323 - 310 = 13 gm. This excess comes only from bags with 11 gm coins, each contributing 1 extra gm per coin picked.
steps.note: The excess equals the sum of picks from 11gm bags: sum of (picks from 11gm bags) = 13.

steps.title: Decode which bags have 11 gm coins
steps.formula_raw: excess = sum of 2^(k-1) for each 11gm bag k; 13 = 8 + 4 + 1 = 2^3 + 2^2 + 2^0
steps.apply: Decompose 13 in terms of available picks {1, 2, 4, 8, 16}: 13 = 8 + 4 + 1. Pick 1 = bag 1, pick 4 = bag 3, pick 8 = bag 4. So bags 1, 3, and 4 have 11 gm coins. Note: 13 cannot be expressed differently using distinct elements from {1,2,4,8,16} — this decomposition is unique.
steps.note: The uniqueness is guaranteed because picks are distinct powers of 2 (binary encoding principle).

steps.title: Compute the product of labels
steps.apply: Bags with 11gm coins are labeled 1, 3, and 4. Product of their labels = 1 * 3 * 4 = 12.

solution.result: The product of the labels of the bags having 11 gm coins is 12.

questions.formula_note: Binary representation trick: picks are distinct powers of 2 so excess weight uniquely identifies which bags have 11gm coins, like reading binary digits.

questions.question:
Consider a sequence of 14 elements: A = [-5, -10, 6, 3, -1, 2, 13, 4, -9, -1, 4, 12, -3, 0]. Determine the maximum of S(i, j), where S(i, j) = sum_{k=i}^{j} A[k] and 0 <= i <= j <= 13. (Divide and conquer approach may be used.)

Answer: ___________
questions.to_find: The maximum subarray sum S(i,j) of the given 14-element sequence

understand.plain: We have a sequence of 14 numbers and need to find the contiguous subarray (from index i to j) that has the largest possible sum. This is the classic 'maximum subarray problem.' Kadane's algorithm solves it in O(n) time. The divide-and-conquer approach mentioned in the question also works in O(n log n).

keywords.explain: Find indices i and j such that the sum of elements from A[i] to A[j] is maximized over all possible 0 <= i <= j <= n-1.
keywords.example: In [-2, 1, -3, 4, -1, 2, 1, -5, 4], the maximum subarray is [4,-1,2,1] with sum 6.

keywords.explain: Scan left to right, maintaining 'max_ending_here' (best sum ending at current index) and 'max_so_far' (global best). At each step: max_ending_here = max(A[i], max_ending_here + A[i]).
keywords.example: For A=[−2,1,−3,4]: steps give max_ending_here = −2,1,−2,4 and max_so_far = −2,1,1,4.

text-anchor='middle'>12</text><text x='675' y='100' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>13</text><text x='350' y='140' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Subarray A[2..11] = [6,3,-1,2,13,4,-9,-1,4,12]</text><text x='350' y='165' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Sum = 6+3-1+2+13+4-9-1+4+12 = 33</text><text x='350' y='190' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>Maximum understand.visual_alt: Array of 14 elements with indices 0-13. The maximum subarray spans indices 2 to 11 (elements 6,3,-1,2,13,4,-9,-1,4,12), shown highlighted, with sum = 33.

given.aim: Find the maximum contiguous subarray sum in the 14-element sequence A.

terms.connects: The large positive elements in the middle (6,3,2,13,4,4,12) form the maximum subarray.

terms.term: S(i,j) = sum from k=i to j of A[k]
terms.meaning: The sum of the contiguous subarray from index i to index j.
terms.connects: We want to maximize S(i,j) over all valid 0<=i<=j<=13.

terms.meaning: max_ending_here tracks best sum ending at current index; reset to current element if accumulated sum goes negative.
terms.example: At A[0]=-5: max_ending_here=-5; at A[2]=6: max_ending_here=max(6, -10+6)=6.
terms.connects: Ensures we find the global maximum subarray in one linear scan.

given.plan: Apply Kadane's algorithm: scan left to right tracking 'max_ending_here' and 'max_so_far'. When max_ending_here drops below the current element, restart from the current element. The final max_so_far is the answer.

steps.title: Apply Kadane's algorithm
steps.formula_raw: max_ending_here = max(A[i], max_ending_here + A[i]); max_so_far = max(max_so_far, max_ending_here)
steps.note: meh = max_ending_here, msf = max_so_far. The maximum is achieved at the subarray A[2..11].

steps.title: Verify the maximum subarray
steps.note: The subarray runs from index 2 (value 6) to index 11 (value 12), spanning 10 elements.

solution.result: The maximum of S(i,j) is 33 (achieved by the subarray A[2..11] = [6, 3, -1, 2, 13, 4, -9, -1, 4, 12]).

questions.formula_note: Kadane's algorithm for maximum subarray sum: O(n) time by tracking max_ending_here and max_so_far in a single left-to-right pass.
